worth_it_explanation: First-party audience signals are how a publisher stays addressable as third-party cookies fall away, so whether they reach curated demand decides how much of the inventory clears at a targeted rate rather than as undifferentiated supply. The check reviews how Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and audience data are passed through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) and whether that data stays inside the consent the user granted, so it costs reading time against the current setup rather than a rebuild. The finding points to a concrete identity and consent decision the publisher can act on.
intro: APS routes a publisher's bid requests to demand through Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace, and curated demand increasingly targets on the publisher's own first-party signals rather than third-party cookies. This subcategory covers the PPID and the audience data a publisher passes alongside it, and whether that data reaches demand in a usable form while staying inside the consent each user granted. These checks confirm that the first-party signal is actually present in the request and that the audience data passed never exceeds the permission the consent string carries.

audit_checks.title: Publisher Provided ID and first-party audience signals are not passed to curated demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) or first-party audience signal in the request, curated demand has no addressable signal to target on once third-party cookies are absent, so the impression competes as undifferentiated supply. The inventory still sells, but at the open-auction rate rather than the targeted rate the audience could command, which is recurring revenue left on the table rather than a single broken setting.

educate.base:
A PPID is an identifier a publisher generates for its own users and passes into the bid request, so demand can recognize and target a user without relying on a third-party cookie. Alongside it a publisher can attach first-party audience signals, the segments and attributes it has built from its own logged-in or observed data. Amazon Publisher Services (APS) carries these signals through Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace to the demand connected there, and curated demand uses them to decide what an impression is worth.

The point of passing the signal is addressability. As third-party cookies fall away across Safari, Firefox, and Chrome's privacy changes, a request that carries no first-party identifier and no audience data gives demand nothing to recognize the user by. The impression still clears, but it clears as undifferentiated supply at the open rate rather than at the targeted rate an addressable audience can command. The first-party signal is what keeps the inventory recognizable to the demand that pays more for it.

This is also where Amazon Publisher Cloud fits because curation depends on the publisher's signals being present to build against. If the PPID and audience data never reach the request, the curated deals a publisher sets up have nothing to act on, and the inventory falls back to whatever demand can transact without an identity signal.

A few signs point to the first-party signal not reaching demand:
  • The bid request leaves the page or the server carrying no PPID and no first-party audience segments
  • The publisher has built audience segments from logged-in or first-party data but cannot confirm those segments appear in the APS request
  • Curated or deal-based demand set up in Amazon Publisher Cloud is underdelivering, which can point to the signals it targets on being absent
You may want to confirm that the PPID and the first-party audience signals the publisher intends to pass are actually present in the request that reaches demand. One approach is to inspect a live request and trace whether the identifier and segments appear, rather than assuming the configuration carries them.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the strongest first-party signal usually comes from registered or subscribed readers, and the audience segments built from that base are what curated demand pays a premium to reach. You may want to confirm the PPID derived from the logged-in audience and the segments built on it reach the APS request, so the subscriber inventory clears at the addressable rate rather than the open rate.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, first-party signals often come from purchase and browsing behavior, which build high-intent segments that curated demand values. You may want to confirm those intent-based segments and the PPID reach the request, so the commercial inventory stays addressable to the demand willing to pay for it rather than falling back to undifferentiated supply.

investigate.intro: Inspect a live bid request through Amazon Publisher Services to confirm whether a Publisher Provided Identifier and first-party audience segments are present, and check whether curated demand has signals to target on.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open the site in a browser, open developer tools, and select the 'Network' tab.
Load a page that serves Amazon Publisher Services (APS) inventory and find the request that carries the bid, then inspect its payload.
Look for a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and any first-party audience segments in the request, and record whether they are present or absent.
Repeat on a logged-in session if the site has one because the first-party signal often depends on the user being recognized.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the APS account and review where the PPID and first-party audience signals are configured to pass into Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace.
Check Amazon Publisher Cloud for any curated deals that target first-party segments, and note whether those segments are defined and delivering.
Record whether the configuration says the signals should pass and whether the live request in the browser confirms they actually do.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and first-party audience segments appear in a live Amazon Publisher Services request, and whether curated demand set up in Amazon Publisher Cloud has signals to target on. A setup where the intended PPID and segments reach the request passes. A setup where the request carries no first-party identifier or no audience data, while the publisher intends to pass them, is a fail recorded with the signals that are missing and where the gap occurs.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a PPID and first-party audience segments appear in a live request above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} passes no Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and no first-party audience signals into its Amazon Publisher Services (APS) requests, so curated demand has nothing to recognize the user by once third-party cookies are absent. The inventory still clears, but it clears as undifferentiated supply at the open rate rather than at the targeted rate an addressable audience can command. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the PPID and the first-party segments the publisher has built actually reach the request, by inspecting a live request rather than assuming the configuration carries them, so the inventory stays addressable to the demand that pays more for it. After the change, recheck a live request to confirm the identifier and segments are present.

audit_checks.title: Audience data passed exceeds the consent the user granted
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Passing a Publisher Provided Identifier or audience segments for a user who did not consent to that use turns an addressability feature into a compliance exposure under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and US state privacy laws. The risk is regulatory and reputational rather than a revenue trade, and it scales with every request that carries data beyond its permission, so the gap compounds rather than staying contained.

educate.base:
Passing a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and first-party audience signals is only safe inside the permission the user granted. A consent management platform records that permission as a consent string, the IAB Europe Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) string under GDPR, or a US state string carried by the Global Privacy Platform (GPP). The question this check asks is whether the audience data Amazon Publisher Services (APS) sends to demand stays inside what that string actually allows.

The failure mode is a mismatch between what gets passed and what was permitted. A user who declined the purpose that covers building a profile or sharing data with third parties should not have a PPID or audience segments attached to their request, yet a setup that passes the signal unconditionally sends it for every user regardless of their choice. The signal that makes the inventory addressable becomes a record of data moving beyond its permission, which is the exposure under GDPR and laws such as the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA).

The fix is to gate the signal on the consent string rather than passing it by default. The consent string is available at request time, so the publisher can read the relevant purposes and suppress the PPID and audience data for any user whose string does not permit it. This keeps the addressability benefit for consenting users while keeping non-consenting users' data out of the bid stream entirely.

A few signs point to audience data exceeding consent:
  • The PPID and audience segments pass on every request with no check against the TCF or GPP consent string first
  • Users who declined the relevant purpose still have a first-party identifier attached to their request
  • No documented rule maps which consent signals must be present before the PPID and segments are allowed to pass
You may want to confirm that the audience data passed is scoped to the consent each user granted. One approach is to test a request under a declined-consent state and verify that no PPID or audience segment appears in it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site serving readers across regions, the same page can fall under GDPR for a European reader and a US state law for a domestic one, so the consent string and the rules that gate the signal differ by user. You may want to confirm the PPID and audience data are suppressed when the reader's TCF or GPP string declines the relevant purpose, rather than passing on a single rule that ignores the regional difference.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the intent-based segments built from purchase and browsing behavior are sensitive precisely because they describe what a user is shopping for, so passing them without consent carries more exposure. You may want to confirm those segments are gated on the consent string and held back for any user who declined the purpose that covers them.

investigate.intro: Test bid requests under different consent states to confirm that a Publisher Provided Identifier and audience segments pass only when the consent string permits, and review the rule that gates them.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open the site, accept consent in the consent management platform, and inspect a live Amazon Publisher Services (APS) request in the 'Network' tab for the Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and audience segments.
Clear the consent state, decline the relevant purpose, reload, and inspect the request again.
Compare the two requests and record whether the PPID and segments are suppressed under declined consent or whether they pass regardless.
Where the site serves multiple regions, repeat under a Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) string and a Global Privacy Platform (GPP) US state string to cover both.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Review where the PPID and audience signals are configured to pass and whether that configuration reads the consent string before sending.
Find the documented rule mapping which consent purposes must be present before the signal is allowed, and note whether one exists.
Record whether the configuration gates the signal on consent or passes it unconditionally.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, across consent states, whether the PPID and audience segments pass only when the TCF or GPP string permits, and whether a documented rule maps consent to the signal. A setup that suppresses the identifier and segments under declined consent and passes them only when permitted passes. A setup that passes the signal unconditionally, or passes it for users who declined the relevant purpose, is a fail recorded with the consent state where data exceeded permission.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the PPID and segments are suppressed under declined consent above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} passes a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) and audience segments without checking the consent string first, so the signal reaches Amazon Publisher Services (APS) demand even for users who declined the relevant purpose. Passing audience data beyond the permission the user granted is an exposure under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and US state laws such as the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), and it scales with every request. {{voice_recommend}} gating the PPID and segments on the Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) or Global Privacy Platform (GPP) string available at request time, so the data passes only when consent permits it. After the change, test a request under a declined-consent state to confirm no PPID or audience segment appears.
